description: Ship combat: hull, shields, modules, weapons, damage

entries.content: A ship's hull integrity is reported in journal events as a float between 0.0 (destroyed) and 1.0 (full health). The HullDamage event fires on each damage tick. At 0.0 the ship is destroyed and the commander incurs the rebuy cost.
entries.source: in-game journal observation
entries._justification: Phase 1 seed entry. Scale confirmed by journal observation -- Health field is normalized 0.0-1.0, not percent.

entries.content: The in-game HUD displays a hull warning when hull integrity drops below 25% (0.25 normalized). At this level the ship's status panel shows amber and voice alert fires. This is the threshold for HULL_CRITICAL_25 broadcast.
entries.source: in-game observation, cockpit HUD behavior
entries._justification: Week 7. Used by Week 9 Hull Triggers as the HULL_CRITICAL_25 threshold. Value 0.25 (normalized) confirmed by cockpit HUD amber transition.

entries.content: The in-game HUD displays a critical hull warning when hull integrity drops below 10% (0.10 normalized). At this level canopy cracks appear and cockpit damage effects increase. This is the threshold for HULL_CRITICAL_10 broadcast.
entries.source: in-game observation, cockpit HUD behavior
entries._justification: Week 7. Used by Week 9 Hull Triggers as the HULL_CRITICAL_10 threshold. Value 0.10 (normalized) confirmed by cockpit damage visual onset.

entries.content: Shields are reported via the Flags field in Status.json -- bit 3 (value 8) is set when shields are up and clear when shields are down. The journal also fires ShieldsDown and ShieldsUp events. Shield strength percentage is not directly in Status.json in all game versions; the Flags bit is the reliable cross-version indicator.
entries.source: Elite Dangerous journal documentation, in-game observation
entries._justification: Week 7. Needed by Week 9 SHIELDS_DOWN handler. Documents the Flags bit 3 approach used in Phase 1 status_reader.py.

entries.content: When a ship is destroyed, the commander can rebuy it for 5% of the ship's total insured value (hull + modules). The rebuy cost appears in the Died journal event payload as the field 'PilotName' is absent and rebuy fields may vary by game version. The standard insurance rate is 5%. Premium insurance (if purchased) reduces this.
entries.source: Elite Dangerous wiki, in-game Died event observation
entries._justification: Week 7. Foundation for Week 10 Rebuy Calculator. Standard insurance rate 5% confirmed from in-game store and community documentation.

entries.content: The standard rebuy cost in Elite Dangerous is 5% of the total insured value of the ship (hull + all fitted modules). The Rebuy Calculator uses this as the default rate. A commander can purchase premium insurance for a lower rate, but that data is not available in the journal -- Phase 2 uses 5% as the default.
entries.source: Elite Dangerous in-game insurance screen, community verification
entries._justification: Week 7. Rebuy Calculator (Feature 11, Week 10) reads this value. 5% is the universally documented standard rate.

entries.content: A fuel level below 25% of tank capacity is considered low. At this level the in-game HUD begins showing a fuel warning. OmniCOVAS broadcasts FUEL_LOW when fuel_main crosses below 25% of fuel_capacity in a downward direction. Expressed as a fraction: 0.25.
entries.source: in-game HUD observation, Phase 1 status_reader.py FUEL_LOW_THRESHOLD
entries._justification: Week 7. Matches the Phase 1 StatusReader FUEL_LOW_THRESHOLD = 0.25 constant so both the legacy sub-event and Phase 2 FUEL_LOW broadcast fire at the same level.

entries.content: A fuel level below 10% of tank capacity is considered critical. At this level the ship may not have sufficient fuel for another jump depending on jump range and fuel per jump. OmniCOVAS broadcasts FUEL_CRITICAL when fuel_main crosses below 10% of fuel_capacity in a downward direction. Expressed as a fraction: 0.10.
entries.source: in-game observation, community consensus on fuel emergency protocols
entries._justification: Week 7. 10% chosen as the critical threshold because below this level a ship is likely unable to complete even a minimum-range jump in some builds. Conservative and actionable.

entries.content: Ship heat level is reported in Status.json as a float 0.0-1.0 (1.0 = 100% heat). Heat above 0.80 (80%) causes module damage over time and triggers OmniCOVAS HEAT_WARNING broadcasts.
entries.source: Elite Dangerous heat mechanics, in-game observation of module damage onset
entries._justification: Week 9. Used by heat_management.py as the HEAT_WARNING broadcast threshold. 0.80 is the community-documented damage onset. Medium confidence: exact damage rate varies by module type.

entries.content: Heat above 0.95 (95%) causes rapid module damage and risks canopy failure. At this level the ship's status panel shows red heat indicators. OmniCOVAS uses 0.95 as the critical tier within HEAT_WARNING broadcasts.
entries.source: Elite Dangerous heat mechanics, in-game observation of critical heat visual indicators
entries._justification: Week 9. Used by heat_management.py rule evaluator to select critical-tier rule text when heat exceeds 0.95. Distinct from heat_warning_threshold (0.80).

entries.content: Heat values above 1.0 (100%) indicate active heat damage to the ship. Values above 1.20 (120%) are observed during fuel scoop overheat and represent extreme thermal stress. The game clips display at 100% but the underlying float can exceed 1.0.
entries.source: Elite Dangerous heat mechanics, fuel scoop overheat observation
entries._justification: Week 9. Documented for completeness. heat_management.py currently triggers at 0.80 and 0.95; 1.20 is reserved for future Phase 2.5 overheat detection.

entries.content: When heat exceeds 0.80 and the 10-sample rolling window trend is rising, the commander should reduce throttle and move away from heat sources. This is the default warning-tier suggestion.
entries.source: Elite Dangerous pilot community guidance, thermal management best practices
entries._justification: Week 9. Used as suggestion text when heat > 0.80 and trend=rising. Referenced by _RULE_HIGH_RISING constant in heat_management.py.

entries.content: When heat exceeds 0.95, immediate action is required: deploy a heat sink or take evasive action to reduce heat exposure. At this level module damage accelerates rapidly.
entries.source: Elite Dangerous pilot community guidance
entries._justification: Week 9. Used as suggestion text when heat > 0.95. Referenced by _RULE_CRITICAL constant in heat_management.py. Highest-priority rule — evaluated before others.

entries.content: When heat is above 0.80 and the trend is steady (not rising or falling), the commander should investigate which modules are generating sustained heat. Likely sources: fuel scoop near limit, heat-generating engineered modules, or afterburner use.
entries.source: Elite Dangerous thermal management guidance
entries._justification: Week 9. Default steady-high-heat rule. Low confidence because cause varies by loadout. needs_review=true until common cause patterns are documented per ship type.

entries.content: When heat is between 0.70 and 0.80 and the trend is rising, the commander should prepare thermal management before the warning threshold is reached.
entries.source: Elite Dangerous thermal management guidance
entries._justification: Week 9. Pre-warning advisory rule. Not currently used by heat_management.py threshold logic (broadcasts only at 0.80+), but documented for Phase 2.5 expansion.

entries.content: When heat was above 0.80 and is now falling below it, thermal management is working. No immediate action required — maintain current behavior.
entries.source: Elite Dangerous thermal management guidance
entries._justification: Week 9. Used as suggestion text when heat crosses downward below 0.80. Provides positive feedback that cooling is effective.

entries.content: A ship module's health is reported in the Loadout journal event as a float between 0.0 (destroyed) and 1.0 (intact). OmniCOVAS broadcasts MODULE_DAMAGED when any module's health is below 0.8 (80%). At this level the module's performance begins to degrade noticeably. Expressed as a fraction: 0.8.
entries.source: Elite Dangerous module damage mechanics, in-game observation of module performance degradation onset
entries._justification: Week 8. Used by Feature 2 Module Health as the MODULE_DAMAGED threshold. 0.8 chosen as the standard community-documented degradation onset. Medium confidence: exact degradation percentage varies by module type.

entries.content: A ship module with health below 0.2 (20%) is critically damaged and at risk of failure. OmniCOVAS broadcasts MODULE_CRITICAL when any module drops below this level. At or near 0.0 the module is destroyed and non-functional. Expressed as a fraction: 0.2.
entries.source: Elite Dangerous module damage mechanics, in-game observation of module failure threshold
entries._justification: Week 8. Used by Feature 2 Module Health as the MODULE_CRITICAL threshold. 0.2 chosen as the point where module failure risk is high and immediate repair is advised. Medium confidence: failure probability at this health level is build-dependent.

entries.content: The Cargo journal event includes a Vessel field that indicates which context owns the cargo: Ship, SRV, or Suit. OmniCOVAS Pillar 1 tracks Ship cargo only. Events where Vessel is SRV or Suit must be dropped. Events where Vessel is absent (older game versions) are treated as Ship context.
entries.source: Elite Dangerous journal documentation, in-game observation of Cargo events across ship/SRV/Suit contexts
entries._justification: Week 8. Cargo handler must filter on Vessel == Ship to avoid polluting ship cargo_inventory with SRV or Suit cargo counts. Observed Vessel values: Ship, SRV, Suit.

entries.content: The Cargo journal event is the canonical source for a ship's cargo inventory. It fires at session start and whenever cargo changes (buy, sell, collect, eject). The Inventory array contains entries with Name (commodity internal name, lowercase) and Count (integer unit count). The top-level Count field is the total units across all commodities.
entries.source: Elite Dangerous journal documentation, in-game cargo transaction observation
entries._justification: Week 8. Documents the Cargo event structure used by cargo.py. Name field uses internal lowercase commodity names (e.g. gold, not Gold) matching the journal output directly.

entries.content: WANTED status in Elite Dangerous is per star system. A commander earns a bounty or wanted flag within a specific system. Jumping to a different system via FSDJump clears the wanted flag for the previous system. OmniCOVAS tracks this with state.is_wanted_in_system, which is set True on CommitCrime or Bounty events and cleared on FSDJump to a new StarSystem.
entries.source: Elite Dangerous crime and bounty mechanics, in-game observation
entries._justification: Week 9. Used by extended_events.py handle_fsd_jump to clear is_wanted_in_system when StarSystem differs from last known system. Prevents stale wanted state persisting across system jumps.

entries.content: The FSDJump journal event fires on arrival in the destination system and contains the destination StarSystem, SystemAddress, StarPos, and Population. StartJump fires when the FSD begins charging (supercruise entry) and does not contain destination system data. OmniCOVAS broadcasts FSD_JUMP only on FSDJump (arrival). StartJump is handled by fuel.py for jump logging only.
entries.source: Elite Dangerous journal documentation, in-game journal observation
entries._justification: Week 9. Prevents extended_events.py from registering on StartJump by mistake. FSDJump carries destination data; StartJump does not. Extended events feature cares about arrival, not departure.

entries.content: The seven core ship module slots are: PowerPlant, MainEngines, FrameShiftDrive, LifeSupport, PowerDistributor, Radar, FuelTank. These slots are present on all flyable ships. They do not follow the SmallHardpointN naming pattern used for optional and hardpoint slots.
entries.source: Elite Dangerous Loadout journal event observation, module slot naming documentation
entries._justification: Week 9. Used by CLAUDE.md Pattern 3 ModuleInfo.json caveat: presence of FrameShiftDrive slot indicates ship context, not SRV/on-foot.

entries.content: Elite Dangerous hardpoint slots come in five sizes: Tiny, Small, Medium, Large, and Huge. Tiny slots are exclusive to utility mounts (heat sinks, chaff, shield boosters). Small through Huge slots hold weapons scaled to ship class.
entries.source: Elite Dangerous outfitting documentation, in-game slot inspection
entries._justification: Week 9. Supports future Pillar 6 module analysis. Hardpoint size determines weapon compatibility and power draw scaling.

entries.content: In the Loadout journal event, hardpoint slots follow the pattern SizeHardpointN where Size is one of Tiny, Small, Medium, Large, Huge and N is a 1-based index. Examples: TinyHardpoint1, SmallHardpoint1, MediumHardpoint1. Optional internal slots follow OptionalInternalN or Slot_SizeN patterns.
entries.source: Elite Dangerous Loadout journal event observation
entries._justification: Week 9. Used by loadout.py and module_health.py to identify slot types from the Slot field in Loadout.Modules entries.

entries.content: A module with health = 0.0 in the Loadout journal event is destroyed and non-functional. The game removes it from the loadout display. Values between 0.0 (exclusive) and 0.2 are critically damaged but still present. OmniCOVAS broadcasts MODULE_CRITICAL for health < 0.2; health = 0.0 indicates destruction.
entries.source: Elite Dangerous module damage mechanics, Loadout event observation post-combat
entries._justification: Week 9. Boundary condition for module_health.py: health == 0.0 still triggers MODULE_CRITICAL (< 0.2). Documented to prevent confusion with module_health_critical_threshold.

entries.content: After a ShieldsDown event, most ships require approximately 30 seconds before shields begin regenerating. The exact time depends on shield generator class and engineering. ShieldsUp fires when regeneration completes. OmniCOVAS tracks shield state via ShieldsDown (state.shield_up=False) and ShieldsUp (state.shield_up=True) journal events.
entries.source: Elite Dangerous shield mechanics, in-game observation across multiple shield generator classes
entries._justification: Week 9. Reference for future SHIELDS_UP broadcast latency. 30s is approximate; exact timing requires per-ship testing. needs_review=true.

entries.content: Common SYS/ENG/WEP pip configurations as [SYS, ENG, WEP] on the 0-8 scale (total always 12): balanced=[4,4,4], combat_shields=[6,2,4], evasive=[2,6,4], full_weapons=[0,4,8], full_shields=[8,0,4]. These are starting points; actual optimal distribution varies by ship and engagement type.
entries.source: Elite Dangerous pilot community documentation, combat guide references
entries._justification: Week 9 Part G. Stored for Phase 4 pip recommendation feature. Data only in Phase 2 — no enforcement. Medium confidence; optimal configurations are build-dependent.
